coredata.dc:title: Doxorubicin delivery to breast cancer cells with transferrin-targeted carbon quantum dots: An in vitro and in silico study
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Drug Delivery Science and Technology
coredata.dc:creator: Mahani, Mohamad
Pourrahmani-Sarbanani, Maryam
Yoosefian, Mehdi
Divsar, Faten
Mousavi, Seyedeh Maral
Nomani, Alireza
coredata.dc:description: The proficient and targeted delivery (TD) of therapeutic drugs is a significant challenge in the clinical healing of cancer. In this study, highly luminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized via citric acid carbonization and modified by transferrin (TF) to improve the water-solubility and increase the interaction ability with target cell receptors. The CQDs based nanocarrier was developed for the imaging and the TD of doxorubicin (Dox) as a model of anti-breast cancer drug. The physicochemical properties of Dox-loaded TF-CQDs were characterized using TEM, UV–Vis and FT-IR spectrophotometry, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The cytotoxicity potentiality of the nanocarrier was investigated by the MTT test across MCF-7 cell lines which exhibited more decrease in cell viability than TF-CQDs or Dox alone. The efficiency of the in vitro release was investigated in the optimum conditions of pH and the release time. The cellular uptake study revealed that TF-CQDs was more efficiently endocytosed by the MCF-7 cells than Dox. The Dox-TF conjugate was more toxic than Dox due to its overcome on multidrug resistance of cancer cells. Furthermore, the binding energy between Dox and TF-CQDs were investigated employing the molecular docking (Mdoc) approach. It was shown that Dox-TF-CQDs nano-carrier can be an excellent TD system for the therapy of breast cancer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Doxorubicin
Breast cancer
Transferrin targeting
Multidrug resistance
Molecular docking

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Drug Delivery Science and Technology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The effect of pH on the in vitro drug release

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) CQDs (B) Dox loaded TF-CQDs.
The FT-IR spectra of the CQDs.
UV–Vis spectra of CQDs, TF, Dox, TF-CQDs and Dox loaded TF-CQDs in the range of 230–650 nm.
Photoluminescence spectrum of CQDs with excitation at 330, 340, 350, 360 and 380 nm.
Fluorescence spectra of CQDs, TF, Dox, and the Dox-loaded TF-CQDs in the range of 400–700 nm with 380 nm excitation. Dox-TF free energy calculation and drug loading efficiency.

Mdoc analysis of interaction between TF and Dox. TF is in green ribbon form in the figure and while brown sticker presents Dox molecule. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The 2D schematic of the hydrophobic interactions and hydrogen bond the amino acids of TF and Dox (dash lines show hydrogen bonds, spiked residues form hydrophobic interactions with Dox).

Drug release from carrier (Dox-TF-CQDs) in phosphate buffer saline at normal and tumor tissue's pH (pH = 7.4 and 5.2). The error bars indicate the standard deviation for triplicate experiments. Cytotoxicity of the Dox-loaded TF-CQDs nanocarrier.
In vitro cytotoxicity of different volumes of CQDs, TF-CQDs, free Dox and Dox-loaded TF-CQDs for 24 h on MCF-7 cells. The error bars indicate the standard deviation for triplicate experiments.
Fluorescence image of cells (MCF-7) after 3 h' incubation with CQDs on left side and Dox loaded TF-CQDs on right side. λex = 380 nm and magnification is 40 × .

head.ce:title: Doxorubicin delivery to breast cancer cells with transferrin-targeted carbon quantum dots: An in vitro and in silico study

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Biomaterials, School of Pharmacy, Zanjan University of Medical Sciences, Zanjan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutics Biomaterials, School of Pharmacy, Zanjan University of Medical Sciences, Zanjan, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The proficient and targeted delivery (TD) of therapeutic drugs is a significant challenge in the clinical healing of cancer. In this study, highly luminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized via citric acid carbonization and modified by transferrin (TF) to improve the water-solubility and increase the interaction ability with target cell receptors. The CQDs based nanocarrier was developed for the imaging and the TD of doxorubicin (Dox) as a model of anti-breast cancer drug. The physicochemical properties of Dox-loaded TF-CQDs were characterized using TEM, UV–Vis and FT-IR spectrophotometry, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The cytotoxicity potentiality of the nanocarrier was investigated by the MTT test across MCF-7 cell lines which exhibited more decrease in cell viability than TF-CQDs or Dox alone. The efficiency of the in vitro release was investigated in the optimum conditions of pH and the release time. The cellular uptake study revealed that TF-CQDs was more efficiently endocytosed by the MCF-7 cells than Dox. The Dox-TF conjugate was more toxic than Dox due to its overcome on multidrug resistance of cancer cells. Furthermore, the binding energy between Dox and TF-CQDs were investigated employing the molecular docking (Mdoc) approach. It was shown that Dox-TF-CQDs nano-carrier can be an excellent TD system for the therapy of breast cancer.

ce:section.ce:para: The most frequent carcinoma among females aged over 50 in the whole worldwide is breast cancer [6]. Typically, the cancer may occur in the lobules, the ducts, and the connective tissues of the breast. Doxorubicin (Dox) hydrochloride, an anthracycline antibiotic, is extensively applied in many kinds of cancers includes breast carcinoma [17,22]. On the other hand, doxorubicin is mainly toxic and teratogenic for the kidneys and heart [8,13]. Unfortunately, conventional chemotherapeutics do not have sufficient differentiation ability between normal and cancer cells and this can be dangerous. The recent developments have supplied more efficient and specific treatment protocol in cancer patients. Targeted therapies are generally less likely to harm normal and healthy cells than chemotherapy [20]. The designing of a site-specific drug delivery system affected by several factors involves efficient vehicles for delivery, the protection of bioactivity of the drug, and improvement of the loading and targeting [12,33]. The drug dosage can be reduced due to excellent delivery efficiency using specifically modified nanomaterials for targeting cancer cells [21].

Carbon-based nanoparticles have concerned attention because of their ability to attach to several drugs and/or ligands [2,30–32,35]. The unique properties of these nanoparticles are their biocompatibility, nontoxic nature, active surface, good conductivity, high chemical stability, and easy synthesis in high quantities at low cost. In cancer therapy, fairly simple forms of drug delivery are usually applied in which carbon-based nanoparticles are modified for specific targeting of a receptor in the tumor tissue [29].

CQDs are a class of quantum dots that are suitable candidates for the biological applications such as targeted drug delivery systems and imaging due to their fluorescence properties and active chemical modification surface for targeting the polymers, organic and inorganic materials and proteins. Many studies have been carried out on applying CQDs for targeting different types of cancers, with the promising result [26,37]. Zheng et al. linked the oxaliplatin (IV) anticancer drug to the surface of CQDs through chemical bonds to integrate the oxaliplatin anticancer activity and CQDs tracking property for simultaneous therapy and tracking [36]. Sharker et al. applied CQD-paclitaxel and CQD-hyaluronan for near-IR imaging of tumor and simultaneously pH sensitive delivery and targeted therapy [23]. Yang et al. developed peptide modified CQDs for nuclear localization signal to transfer Dox into lung tumor cells to enhance anticancer activity [28]. They also studied the efficiency of the conjugation for in vivo therapy and superior inhibition ability compared to dox.

CQDs are becoming a common platform for connecting the receptors beside a drug in an nano-delivery approach. The overexpression of certain receptors for instance, folic acid, transferrin (TF), glycoprotein, monoclonal antibody, and integrins in various kinds of cancers, provides selective drug accumulation and penetration at the tumor sites [1,15].

TF (80 kDa molecular weight) is a blood plasma glycoprotein with about 700 amino acids that is mainly synthesized by the liver. The chief role of TF is to transport iron in the circulatory system. The fast growth of cancer cells increases the iron demand and consequently the overexpression of TF receptor. The TF has great potential for targeting of malignant cells [4,5]. In other words, this protein has dedicated receivers that can be connected to the breast cancer cells [27]. Soe et al. developed TF-conjugated polymeric nanoparticle as a Dox delivery system for treatment of breast cancer cells [25]. Nasrollahi et al. established graphene oxide decorated with TF for the delivery of docetaxel to the MCF-7 human breast cancer cell line [18]. TF-conjugated CQDs have shown the internalization and accumulation ability by receptor mediated endocytosis and also inhibition ability toward Hep G2 growth in the liver cancer [10,14]. Transferrin conjugated CQDs with the anticancer drug doxorubicin has been applied for brain cancer cell treatment [15].

In this work green CQDs was synthesized using one pot hydrothermal method. For the targeting ligand, TF was chosen as the case since the TF receptor is overexpressed in multiple cancers. The TF- conjugated CQDs was applied as a nanocarrier for delivery of doxorubicin to the MCF-7 breast cancer cell line. The Dox-loaded TF-CQDs demonstrated superior cytotoxicity and an improved therapeutic effect against the breast cancer. It was also shown that Dox was blindly docked into the crystal structure of TF. Free energy of Dox-TF binding was calculated about −6.0 kcal.mol−1, and it is in close contact (hydrophobic interaction) with Asp592, Tyr317, Cys227, Leu228, Lys241, Asp229 and Lys233 residues; this binding is also supported by four hydrogen bonds (Glu318, Asp240, Glu594 and Lys591).

Transferrin, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimid (NHS), Amicon® ultra centrifugal filter 10 KDa, and doxorubicin hydrochloride were from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (Saint Louis, Missouri, USA). Other chemicals were obtained from Merck Co. (Darmstadt, Germany, www.merckmillipore.com). MCF-7 cells were purchased from cell bank of Pasteur Institute of Iran (Tehran, Iran) and stored in liquid nitrogen.

The electronic absorption and fluorescence spectra of the synthesized CQDs were recorded by carry50 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Varian, Australia) and carry Eclipse spectrofluorimeter (Varian, Australia) respectively. All spectroscopic measurements were carried out in 1 cm quartz cuvettes. The functional groups were qualified using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Bruker TENSOR 27, Germany). Zeta sizer NanoZS90 (Malvern Instruments Ltd, Malvern, Worcestershire, UK) was used for characterization of the size distribution of CQDs at a temperature of 25 °C. The sample preparation was performed using Herolab Higen 21 Centrifuge (Herolab Gmbh, Germany) at 21,000×g and shaker (KS 501 digital, IKAeWerke, Germany). Axioplan 2 Fluorescence microscope was used for imaging (Carl Zeiss, Göttingen, Germany). For cytotoxicity assay BioTek ELx808™ Microplate Reader (BioTek Instruments, USA) was used.

Carbon dots were synthesized by a previously reported green hydrothermal route [38]. The solution of 1-g citric acid in 5 mL double distilled water was transferred to a 50 mL high-pressure reactor (autoclave) for 6 h reaction time at 200 °C. The autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The brown colloidal CQDs was centrifuged at 21000 g for 30 min and stored in a dark place at 4 °C.

CQDs was conjugated with TF as previously described [15]. A volume of 500 μL of the prepared CQDs colloid was dispersed in 2 mL of PBS (pH = 7.4). three mg of EDC was added and the solution was stirred for 30 min at 25 °C with aluminum foil (protection from light). Two mg NHS was added and the solution was stirred for 30 min 500 μL of 1 mg mL−1 of transferrin in PBS was added drop-wise and stirred for 2 h.

For loading of Dox on the prepared TF-CQDs, 750 μL of 2 mg mL−1 Dox in PBS (pH = 7.4) was added to TF-CQDs colloidal solution and stirred at room temperature overnight (with aluminum foil shielding for light protection). The drug-loaded conjugates were separated from free Dox, using Amicon® Ultra-15 Centrifugal Filter (MWCO 10KD) at 4000×g. The amount of the TF-CQDs loaded Dox was indirectly determined from the concentration of the free Dox in the filtrate, using UV–Vis absorbance at the 480 nm. The following equation was used for calculation of the drug:
Percent loading of the drug = 100* (Total amount of the drug (mg) – The amount of the free drug in filtrate)/Total amount of drug (mg)

Total amount of the added drug
( mM )
⋅ The amount of the free drug in the eluent
( mM )
Total amount of the added drug
( mM )
In vitro pH-dependent drug release.

The drug release was studied at body temperature (37 °C) at two different pH (5.8 and 7.4). Samples were taken at different time intervals for up to 24 h, while the solution was stirred and protected from the light exposure. The samples were centrifuged at 4000×g using Amicon® Ultra-15 Centrifugal Filter (MWCO 10 KDa). The Dox concentration in the filtrate was determined by spectrofluorimeter.

The cytotoxicity of CQDs to MCF-7 cells was appraised by a typical colorimetric assay. MCF-7 cells were cultured at ten thousand cells per well density with RPMI supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin/streptomycin in 5% carbon dioxide at 37 °C overnight. The cytotoxicity of the prepared Dox-TF-CQDs was evaluated on MCF-7 cell. MCF-7 cell were cultured in RPMI 1640 (200 μL). Cells were plated in 96-well plates (1 × 104 cells/well). Three volume including 2, 4, and 8 μL of CQDs, TF-CQDs, Dox-TF-CQDs, and Dox (2 mg mL−1) were added to each well and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, 20 μL 5 mg mL−1 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) solution (5 mg mL−1) was added. After 4 h incubation time at 37 °C, the supernatant was discarded. The formazan was dissolved by addition of DMSO to each well (150 μL). The mixture was shaken about 15 min. The microplate reader was used for determination of optical density at 570 nm. The equation:
Cell viability rate = 100* Absorbance of the cells that were treated with carbon quantum dots/Absorbance of the control groups
Was used for determination of cell viability rate.

MCF-7 cells were cultured with RPMI medium containing 10% (v/v) heat inactive FBS, and 1% penicillin/streptomycin solution (in the presence of 5% CO2 at 37 °C to allow 80% confluence in 24 h). Three μL of Dox-TF-CQDs was added to the cells and incubated for 3 h in previous condition. The excess carbon quantum dots were eliminated by triple washing with PBS. The fluorescent images were acquired with a fluorescent microscope.

All measurements were performed in minimum triplicate. Data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation. The one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was applied to analyze the statistical differences. The P values of less than 0.05 were considered as statistically significant differences.

CQDs were prepared by a nontoxic and economical procedure which has been published earlier [38]. In this method, citric acid carbonization approach was used through a hydrothermal method. TF was conjugated to CQDs through a covalent bond to promote cancer targeted therapy. TF can bind to overexpressed TF receptor of the cancer cells and increases the tumor accumulation and improves the pharmacokinetics of the cytotoxic drugs.

After that, to forming a stable drug delivery system, an ionic self-assembled complex of the CQDs and Dox molecules was prepared. In this approach, the negatively charged carboxyl groups of the CQDs can electrostatically bind to the positively charged amines of Dox molecules. The overall scheme of the designed CQD-based Dox nano-system is presented in Scheme 1 .

The size of the CQDs and Dox-loaded TF-CQDs nano-sized system were studied using TEM. The images show spherical and mono-dispersed particles of 2 nm for CQDs (Fig. 1 A) and increasing the size for Dox-TF-CQDs (Fig. 1B). Additionally, the particle size distribution measured by DLS indicated an average size of about 1.5 nm for the synthesized CQDs (Supplementary Fig. S1).

Fig. 2 shows The FT-IR spectra of CQDs and the observed peaks in 3425, 1734 and 1421 cm−1 are due to surface carboxyl groups (our previous report [16]) and were related to the stretching vibrations of OH, CO and CC groups, respectively.

The average number of carboxyl groups on the CQDs was measured by Boehm method [3]. An amount of 17.5 μL of sodium hydroxide could neutralized all of carboxylic acid residues on the CQDs surface (Supplementary Fig. S2). The obtained value, is used for calculation of the EDC and NHS for carboxylate activation [15].

The quantum yields of the carbon quantum dots were computed by slope method, where the quinine hemisulfate solution (dissolved in 0.1 M sulfuric acid; quantum yield = 0.54) was used as a reference. The electronic absorption intensity of the CQDs and quinine sulfate solution for five different concentrations in 380 nm and 310 nm wavelength were determined using UV–Vis spectrophotometer respectively. The corresponding photoluminescence spectra were recorded in 460 nm for same solutions using the 310 nm and 360 nm as excitation wavelength for quinine sulfate and CQDs respectively. The photoluminescence intensities were regressed on the absorbance for five solutions of each material (Figs. S3A and S3B). Finally, the follows equation was used to determine the quantum yield:
Qc = Qr (mc/mr) (ƞ2 c/ƞ2 r)

Where Q is the quantum yield, m is the measured slope of the curves. The subscript r and c are referring to reference and carbon quantum dots, respectively. ηc is the refractive index of the water (solvent of CQDs), and ηr is the refractive index of 0.1 M sulfuric acid (the solvent of quinine sulfate) that the both of them is 1.33. The results indicated a calculated QY about 5.0% for the synthesized CQDs.

TF-CQDs were studied by their electronic absorption and photoluminescence spectra. Fig. 3 shows the Dox, TF, CQDs, TF-CQDs and Dox-TF-CQDs electronic absorption spectra. Transitions from π and non-bonding to π* are related to CC and CO groups of CQDs respectively. These transitions produced broad band peaks in CQDs, TF-CQDs and Dox-TF-CQDs spectra. The characteristic absorption peaks of around 270 and 500 nm for TF and Dox molecules respectively, can also be observed. A successful electrostatic attachment of Dox to the TF-CQDs surface was confirmed by the presence of both characteristic peaks of TF and Dox 270 and 500 nm, respectively) in the Dox-TF-CQDs spectra.

The unique property of CQDs is the quantum confinement effect that gives them great advantages over the organic fluorophore dyes. In Fig. 4 the fluorescence spectra of carbon quantum dots with excitation at 330, 340, 350, 360 and 380 nm are shown. The excitation dependent fluorescence of this dots [24] can be observed in the spectra. The excitation at the various wavelengths exhibits an emission spectrum at specific wavelength which is a reason of the fluorescence property of particles. Moreover, a symmetric spectrum reveals the uniformity of the particles.

Fig. 5 shows the fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs, TF, Dox and Dox-TF-CQDs with 380 nm excitation. The bare CQDs has a high intensity fluorescence emission peak at 460 nm with 380 nm excitation wavelength. TF and Dox do not have fluorescence emission peak at 460 nm with 380 nm excitation wavelength. However, Dox had an emission peak at 580 nm. After the separation of excess CQDs, the fluorescence spectrum of the nano-carrier (Dox-TF-CQDs) showed two maxima at 460 nm and 580 nm related to CQDs and Dox respectively.

In presented study, binding energy between Dox and TF was investigated employing molecular docking (Mdoc) approach. TF-Dox binding strength and residues was studied using Mdoc to obtain an insight into site specific interaction. RCSB Protein Data Bank [PDB: 6CTC] was used as the source of the 3-dimensional crystal structure of TF. Hindrance was avoided by removing of all the water molecules in a bid and the other parameters of docking were as usual. The lowest-energy TF-Dox complex was considered in our study out of 100 generated binding modes. considerable crystallographic works identified extra drug binding sites for the TF receptor, that are used at high drug concentrations. Dox was blindly docked into the crystal structure of TF. As depicted in Figs. 6 and 7
, Dox could bind in the vicinity of TF with −6.0 kcal mol−1 binding free energy, and it is in close contact (hydrophobic interaction) with Asp592, Tyr317, Cys227, Leu228, Lys241, Asp229 and Lys233 residues; this binding is also supported by four hydrogen bonds (Glu318, Asp240, Glu594 and Lys591).

According the literature Dox-TF conjugate is more cytotoxic than Dox, due to its succeed against multidrug resistance of cancer cells [7]. Dox was conjugated TF-CQDs through electrostatic forces [19]. The excess drug molecules were removed by Amicon® Ultra-15 centrifugal filter (10KD). The final average loading efficiency of Dox was 480 nm absorption peak. The loading was calculated 70.37%.

Förster resonance energy transfer is a distance dependent radiationless energy transfer from donor to acceptor [11]. The techniques based on FRET can be used for tracking in addition to sensing without other additional fluorophores. In our probe, each of CQDs and doxorubicin has two rules: carrier and donor for CQDs and drug and acceptor for doxorubicin. Fig. S4 shows that addition of 100–500 μL of 2 mg mL−1 Dox to CQDs results in decrease in emission peak at 460 nm and increase in emission peak at 580 nm. In contrast, the release of Dox from the nanocarrier showed an increase in emission peak at 460 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of pH on the in vitro drug release
ce:section.ce:para: Due to high glycolysis, the extracellular tumor microenvironment exhibits a more acidic pH than the normal tissues [9]. The release of drug from the Dox-TF-CQDs nanocarrier was investigated at pH = 5.2 and 7.4. These two amounts of pH are the representatives of the acidic tumor microenvironment and the normal physiological conditions, respectively. Fig. 8 shows a sharp release of drug followed by slower patter for up to 24 h. At pH = 7.4, the prepared nano-sized system showed a good stability and we found that only 18% of the Dox could be released within 10 h. In contrast, we observed an approximate of 47% of the drug was rapidly released at the first 12 h at pH = 5.2. However, within 24 h, only 50% of Dox was released after the acidic condition and less than 23% at pH = 7.4. Since TF has an isoelectric point (pI) of 5.9, it can be negatively charged under weakly alkaline conditions which make strong electrostatic interaction with the positively charged Dox (pI 8.4) in this condition. When the pH value was reduced to 5.2, the TF and Dox exhibit a positive charge and more drug release was observed.

These results demonstrated that the release rate of the Dox from the system was considerably dependent on the environmental pH values. Lower pH value of tumor tissues in comparison to normal tissues is a very useful characteristic for targeting in cancer therapy.

The cytotoxicity of Dox-loaded TF-CQDs nanocarrier on MCF-7 cells was evaluated by MTT assay. Fig. 9 shows that the cytotoxicity of doxorubicin is increased linearly with the applied dose. ANOVA showed that there is not any significant difference between the toxicity of the TF-CQDs and CQDs but a significantly higher toxicity (P < 0.000001) for Dox and Dox + TF + CQDs compared to CQDs on the cancer cells at the similar Dox concentrations. The results demonstrated that Dox-TF-CQDs nanocarrier at a concentration of 14 mg mL−1 could kill an average of 70% of the MCF-7 cells, while Dox at the same concentration killed about 35% of the cells. However, only a minor cytotoxicity was observed for CQDs and TF-CQDs in the absence of the drug. The significant higher inhibitory effect of nanocarrier could be due to its overcome on multidrug resistance of cancer cells and also targeting by conjugated TF on the surface of the nanocarrier which significantly enhanced the delivery of Dox into the cancer cells through the overexpressed TF receptors.

The transferrin receptor is overexpressed in MCF-7 cells, and can be a good candidate for targeting [34,5]. To evaluate the efficacy of the prepared nano-sized delivery system for the intracellular imaging and in vitro drug delivery, the MCF7 cell uptake of Dox-TF-CQDs was investigated using fluorescent microscopy. Fluorescence microscopic image of MCF7 cells is shown in Fig. 10 . Staining the cancer cells by Dox-loaded TF-CQDs system indicated a high delivery efficacy of drug into the cancer cells. This result suggests that the prepared nanocarrier can efficiently be internalized into MCF-7 cells via transferrin receptor and deliver the Doxorubicin into the cancer cells.

In this study, we successfully developed the Dox loaded TF-CQDs nanocarrier for the effective imaging and the operative targeted delivery of Dox against MCF-7 cell line. The nanocarrier showed a pH-dependent Dox release behavior. In vitro results showed that the amount of released drug was around 50% in the acidic environment whereas less than 23% at pH 7.4 after 24 h. MTT assay on MCF-7 cell demonstrated the enhanced toxicity of Dox loaded TF-CQDs (70%) than Dox which is due to overexpression of transferrin receptor on cancer cells. The fluorescent CQDs provides the tracking potential in the nanocarrier. The results indicate that the Dox loaded TF-CQDs can be applied for the targeted drug delivery.
There are no funders to report for this submission.

Mohamad Mahani: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Maryam Pourrahmani-Sarbanani: Visualization, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Mehdi Yoosefian: Methodology, Visualization, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Faten Divsar: Visualization, Formal analysis, Visualization, Formal analysis. Seyedeh Maral Mousavi: Visualization, Formal analysis. Alireza Nomani: Visualization, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jddst.2021.102342.
